original_abstract: The proliferation of new academic journals in political science, coupled with rising submission rates at existing outlets, has fueled editorial concerns regarding "reviewer fatigue." Many editors operate under the assumption that an overwhelming volume of requests has rendered scholars less willing to undertake the demanding, anonymous task of peer review. However, despite these concerns, there has been no systematic investigation into the specific reasons why scholars decline these invitations. This study provides an empirical analysis of acceptance and refusal rates, as well as the justifications provided by scholars when they decline to review. Our findings suggest that reviewer fatigue is merely one of several factors influencing these decisions. Rather than a lack of professional commitment, the evidence indicates that while scholars remain fundamentally willing to contribute to the peer-review process, their participation is frequently constrained by the competing demands of their professional and personal lives.
humanized_abstract: There are many more journals in political science today, and most have more papers to review than ever before. Editors worry about reviewer fatigue, the assumption being that academics will decline requests to review frequently because they will be asked too often. This paper is the first empirical analysis of the acceptance and refusal rates of reviewer requests and the reasons that academics give for their refusals. It finds that reviewer fatigue is only one of a number of factors that influence an academics' decisions about whether to accept or decline a request to review. While academics are generally willing to review in the sense that they are prepared to do the hard work involved in being a reviewer in the manner described above, there are a host of other factors that mean they are rarely in a position to do so.
